- EU initiatives in telecoms, energy, and transport led to consumer price reductions and productivity improvements.
- Opening energy and telecom markets improved service quality by challenging monopolies.
- PSD2 and open banking are expected to replicate this success by enhancing banking services.
- Open banking is facilitated by open application programming interfaces (APIs), enabling program development for accessing bank customer information with consent.
- APIs ensure reliable system communication, can be internal or external, and lower entry barriers for new businesses by reducing transaction costs.
- The UK's HM Treasury working group on open banking API standards received contributions from nine major banks, influencing API design and promoting standard adoption beyond the UK, e.g., KBC Bank in Ireland.
- The “swagger” software allows developers to construct APIs compatible with bank systems, improving application functionality.
- Network effects, increasing service value with more users, are pivotal but can be diluted by too many service providers or low-quality offerings.
- Balancing open vs. closed API access is crucial, with open access fostering competition and innovation, while closed access may ensure service quality but hinder competition.
- Banking's future may hinge on embracing open banking as a platform, learning from the decline of firms like Nokia and Myspace that resisted external developer contributions.

Technical and Regulatory Sandboxes
In terms of Open Banking, two forms of test environment exist.

The first is technical sandboxes in which third party providers (TPPs) can test their communication with the API connections of a simulated bank or the test environment of a real bank. The second is regulatory sandboxes, which are testing grounds for new business models, that are neither protected by current regulation nor supervised by regulatory institutions.

Technical Sandboxes

In a technical sandbox, the FinTech organisations can test their API connections with a simulated banking environment. Users are typically able to remotely access account information, process transfers, and conduct other standard banking activities without fear of adversely affecting real banking data. As part of PSD2, banks were obliged to offer such test environments to potential FinTech partners, six months ahead of the September 2019 implementation deadline. However, a number of Open Banking related test environments were already available across the globe before this time.

Regulatory Sandboxes

Regulatory sandboxes are testing grounds for new business models that are neither protected by current regulation nor supervised by regulatory institutions. According to the FCA (Financial Conduct Authority, 2017), a regulatory sandbox has the potential to deliver more effective competition in the interests of consumers by:

reducing the time and potentially, the cost of getting innovative ideas to market;

enabling greater access to finance for innovators;

enabling more products to be tested and thus, potentially, be introduced to the market;

allowing regulators to work with innovators, to ensure that appropriate consumer protection safeguards are built into their new products and services.

The FCA has engaged with a number of cohorts of regulatory sandbox participants. In their 2017 report, they reference some of the lessons that have been learnt, while noting that the scheme has been successful, listing the following indicators (Financial Conduct Authority, 2017):

75% of firms accepted into the first cohort, have successfully completed testing.

Around 90% of firms that completed testing in the first cohort, are continuing toward a wider market launch following their test.

The majority of firms issued with a restricted authorisation for their test, have gone on to secure a full authorisation, following completion of their tests.

At least 40% of firms which completed testing in the first cohort, received investment during or following their sandbox tests.

146 applications were received across the first two cohorts of the sandbox. Of these applicants, 50 were accepted and 41 tested or, were at the time testing a range of propositions in the sandbox.

Around a third of firms that tested in the first cohort, used the learnings to significantly pivot their business model ahead of launch in the wider market.

One firm successfully triggered their exit plan, due to lack of consumer uptake during the test.

Reflect

Were you aware of the technical sandbox and regulatory sandbox concepts in advance of this step? Notwithstanding the underlying technical complexity, do you have a better understanding of the role of sandboxes in support of Open Banking?

- Open Banking offers two test environments: technical sandboxes for TPPs to test API communication and regulatory sandboxes for experimenting with new business models outside current regulations.
- **Technical Sandboxes** enable FinTech organizations to interact with simulated or real bank APIs, allowing safe testing of transactions and account access without risking real data. PSD2 mandated banks to provide these environments to FinTech partners by March 2019, promoting innovation.
- **Regulatory Sandboxes** provide a controlled environment for testing innovative financial services that aren't covered by existing regulation. The FCA highlights their benefits, including reduced market entry time and costs, improved financing access for innovators, increased product testing leading to market launches, and building in consumer protection measures.
- Success indicators from the FCA's regulatory sandbox include a high completion rate of testing, continuous progress toward wider market launches, acquisition of full authorizations, and significant investments received by firms post-testing. Learnings from these sandboxes have led to business model pivots and market readiness assessments.
- The existence and function of technical and regulatory sandboxes play a crucial role in fostering the development and integration of innovative solutions in the Open Banking ecosystem.

Open Banking: Expectations vs Reality

Drawing on his extensive experience working in open banking, John provides interesting insights into the reality of the open banking movement against the original expectations. John begins with some context and background on the concept of open banking, while discussing the envisaged opportunities for new commercial services. In the case of non-bank financial institutions, there is a structural distance between institutions and borrowers. Non-bank financial institutions do not have the same scope for developing client relationships given that, as non-depository entities, credit extension over time is the main channel available for building a history with borrowers (Preece and Mullineaux, 1994).

Therefore, non-bank financial institutions are recognised for their due diligence in appraising loan applications, and loan approvals are often seen as strong signals of borrower creditworthiness to the market (Preece and Mullineaux, 1994). Donaldson et al. (2017) further identify the importance of non-bank financial institutions as complementary to commercial banking institutions in an economy, arguing that the presence of non-bank financial institutions in a competitive credit market allows entrepreneurs to pursue funding for higher-risk projects. This is on the basis that non-bank financial institutions have higher hurdle rates to overcome, given their higher cost of capital relative to banking institutions. Despite the relatively higher barriers to alleviating information asymmetries, non-bank financial institutions have long been a source of capital investment (Lee, 1966), with increasing market share particularly observed over the past four decades (Donaldson et al., 2017).

Relatively few studies examine the lending practices of non-bank financial institutions. In the case of large business lending, non-bank financial institutions typically form part of syndicated loans, which are initiated and led by banking institutions (Invashina and Scharfstein, 2010). This creates difficulties in appraising the lending practices of non-bank financial institutions. Petersen and Rajan (1994) use the small business lending environment to provide insights into the differential effects in relationships between banking institutions and borrowers, and non-bank financial institutions and borrowers. Preece and Mullineaux (1994) study the monitoring role of banks and non-banks, concluding that markets respond favourably to loan agreement announcements made by non-banks, and that non-banks operate to similar principles as banks. Other lines of research examine the interaction of bank and non-bank institutions within credit markets (Begenau and Landvoigt, 2017; Donaldson et al., 2017; Martinez-Miera and Repullo, 2017; Ueda, 2004), the influence of non-bank institutions in banking crises (Von Hagen and Ho, 2007), and the role of non-bank institutions in facilitating economic growth (Cheng and Degryse, 2010).

make notes from above .....do not miss anything and do not add extra

- Non-bank financial institutions have a structural distance from borrowers, lacking direct depositor relationships, making credit extension a primary method for building borrower histories (Preece and Mullineaux, 1994).
- They are known for diligent loan application appraisals, with loan approvals signaling borrower creditworthiness to the market (Preece and Mullineaux, 1994).
- Recognized as complementary to commercial banks, they allow for funding high-risk projects due to higher hurdle rates stemming from a higher cost of capital (Donaldson et al., 2017).
- Non-bank financial institutions have been a capital source with an increasing market share over the last forty years (Donaldson et al., 2017).
- Research on non-bank lending practices is limited; often, they participate in syndicated loans led by banking institutions, complicating appraisal of their lending practices (Invashina and Scharfstein, 2010).
- Petersen and Rajan (1994) explore differential relationship effects between banking/non-banking institutions and borrowers, particularly in small business lending.
- Markets respond positively to loan agreements announced by non-banks, operating on principles similar to banks (Preece and Mullineaux, 1994).
- Studies also examine the interaction of bank and non-bank institutions within credit markets, their influence on banking crises, and their role in facilitating economic growth (Various authors).

FinTech and Banking: Friends or Foes?
Navareti et al. (2017) consider the relationship between innovative FinTech companies and incumbent banks. Then consider the question of whether the so-called FinTech revolution will lead to the demise of banking institutions as we know them today or even the collapse of the current banking business model.

The authors conclude that:

“FinTechs enhance competition in financial markets, provide services that traditional financial institutions do less efficiently or do not do at all, and widen the pool of users of such services. But they will not replace banks in most of their key functions.”

It is identified by Navareti et al. (2017) that FinTech companies typically offer similar products and services to traditional banks but in an unbundled way. This unbundling of banking functions necessarily constrains and limits the range of products and services that FinTech companies can offer. This contrasts to traditional banks that are constructed on the basis of bundled banking functions. In this respect, the continued growth of successful FinTech companies may push their underlying business models towards bundled banking functions.

The authors, therefore, envisage that:

“The business model of FinTechs, therefore, is highly likely to gradually converge towards that of banks.”

A consequence of this convergence in the FinTech and bank business models, will be a decline in the extent of regulatory arbitrage. As FinTechs expand their product and service range through bundling, they will increasingly fall under the regulatory radar.

Navareti et al. (2017) further argue the convergence of FinTech and bank business models across a number of dimensions. In respect of funding and revenue structure, lending platform based FinTech companies, for example, that follow a fee-based revenue model will require greater scale and improved management of information asymmetry to ensure sustainability. This will move the FinTech companies away from an “agency model” towards assuming more risks and bundling more and more banking functions. Furthermore, it is argued by Navareti et al. (2017) that as regulators trade-off between fostering competition and protecting the financial system, we may see a regulatory squeeze on the FinTech companies as they grow in size and market share and become ever more systemically important.

make notes from above .....do not miss anything and do not add extra

- FinTech companies enhance competition in financial markets and offer services that traditional banks may not or are less efficient at, widening the service user base.
- Despite their contributions, FinTechs are not expected to replace banks in most key functions.
- FinTechs offer products and services in an unbundled manner, unlike traditional banks that operate on bundled banking functions.
- The growth of FinTechs may lead their business models to gradually converge towards those of traditional banks.
- This convergence reduces regulatory arbitrage as FinTechs begin to bundle more services and fall under increased regulatory scrutiny.
- Funding and revenue structures of FinTechs, especially lending platform-based ones, may evolve to require greater scale and management of information asymmetries, moving away from an agency model towards more risk assumption and service bundling.
- Regulatory pressures on FinTechs are expected to increase as they grow in size, market share, and systemic importance, balancing the trade-off between fostering competition and protecting the financial system.
